whatToFeed: Balanced, water-soluble organic fertilizer or compost tea.
pruningTips: Pinch the purple flower spikes as soon as they appear to extend leaf production and maintain flavor.
flavorProfile: Bold anise, licorice, and sweet clove notes; sturdier and more heat-tolerant than Genovese.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant outdoors late May or early June, well after all danger of frost.
whenToHarvest: Continuous harvest by clipping sprigs down to a leaf node once the plant is 6-8 inches tall.
usesForLargeHarvests: Infusing simple syrups for cocktails, making Thai basil pesto, freezing in oil cubes, stir-fries.
hardinessNote: Grow as annual in Zone 6/7; highly frost-sensitive.
plantingInstructions: Sow seeds indoors 6 weeks before the last frost date, pressing lightly into seed starting mix as they require light to germinate. Move outdoors in late May, spacing plants 12 inches apart once overnight lows consistently stay above 50°F.
growthRequirements: Demands highly fertile, warm, and loose soil with excellent drainage and a neutral pH of 6.0 to 7.0. Requires steady root moisture without waterlogging, combined with intense full sun and protection from cold drafts.

whatToFeed: Light application of a balanced organic fertilizer in spring; thrives in low-fertility soil.
pruningTips: Cut back by one-third in early spring to prevent woodiness; prune lightly after flowering to maintain shape.
flavorProfile: Earthy, classic thyme base layered with a bright, intense lemon aroma and citrus flavor.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant outdoors in mid-to-late May once the soil has warmed completely.
whenToHarvest: Harvest leaves and sprigs as needed throughout the season, ideally in the morning.
usesForLargeHarvests: Drying, infusing in cocktail vinegar or honey, blending into herb butter, freezing in ice trays.
hardinessNote: Treat as an annual or provide heavy winter mulch/protection to overwinter in Zone 6b.
plantingInstructions: Best grown from nursery starts or cuttings, as seeds can be erratic. Set young plants into the garden in mid-May, spacing them 8-12 inches apart. Press the surrounding soil down firmly and water thoroughly to establish.
growthRequirements: Thrives in lean, dry, gritty, or rocky soils with superb drainage and a pH range of 6.5 to 7.5. Highly drought-tolerant once established; excessive moisture or heavy clay soils will trigger root rot rapidly. Requires full sun.

whatToFeed: Balanced liquid seaweed or organic vegetable fertilizer.
pruningTips: Pinch growth tips early in the season to encourage a bushier habit and prevent top-heavy, brittle stems.
flavorProfile: Sweet, tropical, and fruity aroma closely mimicking fresh pineapple, with a mild sage undertone.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant outdoors late May, ensuring night temperatures are safely above 50°F.
whenToHarvest: Snip leaves and tender stem tips regularly all summer before late-fall blooming starts.
usesForLargeHarvests: Muddling for cocktails, infusing into simple syrups, crafting herbal teas, freezing leaves in ice cubes.
hardinessNote: Grows vigorously as an annual in Zone 6/7; dies back completely at first frost.
plantingInstructions: Purchase established nursery starts or propagate from stem cuttings. Plant outside in late May once all spring frost risk is completely gone, leaving 24-36 inches between plants to allow for its massive, wide bushy growth.
growthRequirements: Requires rich, fertile, moist, yet sharply-draining loam with a pH of 6.0 to 7.0. Needs abundant space and consistent root moisture to support fast vegetative growth before it explodes with late-season red blooms. Full sun is essential.

whatToFeed: Rarely needs food; a very light compost top-dress in mid-summer if growth stalls.
pruningTips: Keep the tips pinched back regularly to prevent it from becoming top-heavy and flopping over.
flavorProfile: Peppery, pungent, and sweet; reminiscent of a delicate cross between thyme and marjoram.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Sow seeds directly or transplant after the final spring frost in mid-to-late May.
whenToHarvest: Harvest entire stems just before the plant begins to produce its tiny white-to-pink flowers.
usesForLargeHarvests: Drying for spice blends (Herbes de Provence), flavoring bean dishes, infusing vinegar.
hardinessNote: True annual; completes its life cycle in one season.
plantingInstructions: Directly sow seeds outdoors in mid-May 1/8 inch deep, as they need a bit of light to germinate. Space rows 12 inches apart and thin the emerged seedlings to a final spacing of 6 inches. Water with a fine mist to keep from washing away seeds.
growthRequirements: Thrives in moderately rich, light, sandy, exceptionally well-draining soil with a pH of 6.5 to 7.5. Requires full sun exposure and average, uniform watering. Cannot handle waterlogged or highly compacted soils.

whatToFeed: Thrives in poor soils; do not fertilize unless growing in completely depleted container soil.
pruningTips: Prune back hard by half in early spring to remove old wood and stimulate tender, flavorful new growth.
flavorProfile: Sharp, intensely peppery, and piney; much stronger, warmer, and more pungent than summer savory.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant transplants outdoors in mid-to-late May once frost risks are completely gone.
whenToHarvest: Can be harvested year-round once established, though spring and summer leaves are the most tender.
usesForLargeHarvests: Drying for hearty stews, flavoring roasted root vegetables, curing meats.
hardinessNote: Reliably perennial in Zone 6/7; stays semi-evergreen through Western PA winters.
plantingInstructions: Start seeds indoors 8 weeks before frost, or set out purchased nursery starts in late May. Space plants 10-12 inches apart in a dedicated perennial bed or rock garden, pressing soil firmly around the roots.
growthRequirements: Demands poor, lean, sandy, or gravelly soils with exceptional drainage capability and a pH of 6.5 to 7.5. Highly drought-resistant and prefers dry, hot environments in full sun. Highly sensitive to heavy winter wetness.

whatToFeed: Heavy feeder; requires a high-quality organic liquid fertilizer every two weeks all summer.
pruningTips: Prune frequently by cutting stems back by one-quarter to prevent a leggy, open, woody skeletal structure.
flavorProfile: The most powerful, clean, and intense pure lemon aroma and flavor of any cultivated herb.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Move outdoors late May when overnight lows are consistently above 45°F.
whenToHarvest: Harvest mid-afternoon when essential oils peak by cutting stems just above a leaf node.
usesForLargeHarvests: Infusing alcohol and cocktail syrups, drying for loose leaf teas, making citrus sugars.
hardinessNote: Grows as an annual in Zone 6/7 unless potted and brought indoors before temperatures drop below 40°F.
plantingInstructions: Propagated strictly from nursery containers or soft wood cuttings. Plant outdoors in late May into deep beds or large containers, leaving 18-24 inches of clearance around the base to accommodate wide woody branches.
growthRequirements: Requires rich, loose, highly porous loam with continuous drainage and a pH of 6.0 to 6.5. Demands regular watering but the root system must never sit in stale water. Needs full sun to develop high concentrations of aromatic oils.

whatToFeed: Light application of an organic, low-nitrogen fertilizer to prevent diluted leaf sweetness.
pruningTips: Pinch out stem tips when 8 inches tall to force branching; continuously pinch flower buds to preserve flavor.
flavorProfile: Intensely sweet, sugar-like taste with a faint herbal, licorice-like finish; completely calorie-free.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Set out transplants in late May or early June once soil is thoroughly warm.
whenToHarvest: Peak sweetness occurs in the cool morning autumn temperatures right before flowering buds split open.
usesForLargeHarvests: Drying and grinding into a green sugar powder, boiling into a clear liquid sweetener concentrate.
hardinessNote: Grow as an annual in Zone 6/7; roots cannot tolerate freezing soils without protection.
plantingInstructions: Set out healthy nursery transplants in late May once overnight temperatures stay above 50°F. Space each plant 12 inches apart in rows 18 inches apart. Handle roots gently as they can be brittle during transplanting.
growthRequirements: Prefers light, fluffy, well-aerated sandy loam containing rich organic compost with a pH range of 6.5 to 7.5. Demands consistent, shallow root moisture but will rot instantly in heavy standing clay. Thrives in full sun.
